content:
In the formation-tutorial dialog, make the following changes:

1. Merge the current title into the subtitle. So it is something like "3-Tile - Line Match". Maybe the '3-Tile" in another color, do what you think would be the best UX here? 

2. Replace the current title "3-Tile Match" with a single headline that frames the merge as reaching the next tier — something like "Level Up!" or "Next Tier!" Avoid any language that could be read as a math operation.

3. Add a small tier chain visualization between the merge example and the "Got it!" button, showing the progression 2 → 4 → 8 → 16, with the resulting tile (in this case 8) visually highlighted via a brighter color or subtle glow. This should communicate that numbers are tier labels in a sequence, not values to be added.

4. Add a short explanatory line directly below the merge example, reading something like: "3 matching tiles merge into the next tier." Use the word "tier" instead of any math-implying terms like "value" or "sum". Style it as a small centered subtitle in a muted color.

message.content:
In the formation-tutorial dialog, make the following changes:

1. Merge the current title into the subtitle. So it is something like "3-Tile - Line Match". Maybe the '3-Tile" in another color, do what you think would be the best UX here? 

2. Replace the current title "3-Tile Match" with a single headline that frames the merge as reaching the next tier — something like "Level Up!" or "Next Tier!" Avoid any language that could be read as a math operation.

3. Add a small tier chain visualization between the merge example and the "Got it!" button, showing the progression 2 → 4 → 8 → 16, with the resulting tile (in this case 8) visually highlighted via a brighter color or subtle glow. This should communicate that numbers are tier labels in a sequence, not values to be added.

4. Add a short explanatory line directly below the merge example, reading something like: "3 matching tiles merge into the next tier." Use the word "tier" instead of any math-implying terms like "value" or "sum". Style it as a small centered subtitle in a muted color.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- compare-plans: Compare the last two plan versions in Plan Mode by saving the previous and current plan texts to temporary files and opening them in FileMerge or opendiff. Use when a user wants to inspect how a proposed plan changed between revisions.
- linear: Run `linear $ARGUMENTS` in the shell and use the output as context for the issue. Then briefly su...
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create a scheduled task that can be run on demand or automatically on an interval.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install a matching plugin, try a skill, connect tools.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of the current structure. Let me understand the changes needed:

1. **Merge title into subtitle**: Currently: `h3` = subtitle (e.g., "3-Tile Match"), `h2` = title (e.g., "Line"). The new subtitle should be something like "3-Tile · Line Match" combining both, where "3-Tile" could be in an accent color.

2. **New h2 title**: Replace "3-Tile Match" / "Line" with something like "Level Up!" or "Next Tier!" — I'll go with "Level Up!" as it's more exciting and communicates achievement without math implications.


3. **Tier chain visualization**: I need to add a sequence showing `2 → 4 → 8 → 16` between the merge example and the confirmation button, with the result tier highlighted to show where the merged tiles land in the progression.

4. **Explanatory line**: Add a caption directly below the merge example that reads "3 matching tiles merge into the next tier."

For the header restructuring, I'm updating the HTML to combine the subtitle and title fields into a single merged subtitle, then replacing the main heading with "Level Up!"

—so the new `h2` becomes "Level Up!" and the `h3` becomes something like "3-Tile · Line Match" with the tile count styled separately. This means I need to refactor the `FORMATION_TUTORIAL_DIALOGS` object to support this new format.

Looking at each formation type, the subtitle pattern should be: tile count, a separator, and the formation name—so "3-Tile · Line Match", "4-Tile · Block Match", "5-Tile · T-Formation", and so on.

I could restructure the data to store count and name separately, then combine them in the template with the count wrapped in a span for styling. The current h3 styling already handles the subtitle positioning, so I just need to update what gets rendered there.

Looking at the HTML structure, h3 comes before h2, which means the subtitle displays above the main title. For the new design where "Level Up!" should be the prominent heading, I need to swap the order so h2 appears first, followed by h3 with the formation details below.

Now I'm working on the tier chain visualization that shows the progression of tile values, with the result tile highlighted to indicate what the player achieved. I need to handle edge cases where the tier range goes below 1, so I'll use `max(1, rv-2)` as the starting point and show up to `rv+1`, giving me 3-4 tiles with rv highlighted in context. For example, if v=2 (display 4) and rv=3 (display 8), I'd show the chain 1→2→[3]→4 displaying as 2→4→[8]→16.

Now for the explanatory text, I can generate it dynamically based on the formation type and tile count rather than hardcoding each message. I'll derive the explanation from whether it's a 5-tile formation and use that to determine if tiles merge one tier up or two tiers up. I'm working through the structure for displaying merge rules and tier progression. The key formations show different matching requirements—3 or 4 tiles move up one tier, while 5 tiles skip ahead two tiers. I need to organize this in the dialog with the merge example first, then add an explanatory line right below it, followed by a tier chain visualization before the confirmation button. Let me map out the HTML structure to place the explanatory text inside the formation example after the result card, then add the tier progression display as a separate section below. I'm restructuring `buildFormationContent` to return a wrapper containing both the formation example and the merge description, rather than just the single div it currently returns.

I need to update the goal dialog's flex layout to use `flex-direction: column` and `align-items: center` so these stacked elements display properly.

Restructuring the tutorial dialogs...

Building the tier chain helper function... Writing CSS styles... to be actual tile tier indices (1, 2, 3, 4... for 2, 4, 8, 16...).

I'll use a simple approach: if v=2 (the tile value), rv=3. The chain shows tiers 1→2→3→4, which are display values 2→4→8→16 (using `getDisplayValue`).

Let me verify the existing code: `const rv = v + (is5 ? 2 : 1)` — so for line_3, rv = v+1. For line_5, rv = v+2.

For the tier chain, I want to show a 4-element window centered around rv. I'll display [max(1, rv-2), ..., max(1, rv-2)+3], ensuring rv is visible and highlighted. Testing this: for rv=3, the chain starts at 1 and shows 1→2→[3]→4 (display: 2→4→[8]→16). For rv=5, it starts at 3 and shows 3→4→[5]→6 (display: 8→16→[32]→64). For rv=7 with a 5-tile formation, it starts at 5 and shows 5→6→[7]→8. This looks right.

Now I need to check if the CSS for the goal-dialog-content needs updating...

Wrapping the formation content in its own container...

I'll create a `.formation-tutorial-content` div with flexbox column layout and centered alignment, then add spacing between the sections. This keeps the changes isolated from the main dialog styles. For the tier chain tiles, I'm sizing them at 28px with 12px font to keep them smaller than the formation example tiles, and I'll apply a similar glow effect to the result tiles using box-shadow.

Now I'm ready to implement all the changes: restructuring the tutorial dialogs data format, updating the header template with the "Level Up!" styling, adding the merge description and tier chain sections to the content builder, and writing out all the necessary CSS rules for the new elements.

input.description: Find gem base class in game.css

content.thinking:
The `.gem` class doesn't have a fixed width/height — it uses `aspect-ratio: 1` and the size is determined by the container or parent. The `.formation-example .gem` overrides with explicit `width: 30px, height: 30px`. For `.tier-chain-tile`, I've added explicit `width: 36px, height: 36px` which should work.

The font size is `clamp(16px, 3vw, 24px)` for the base `.gem`. My `.tier-chain-tile` sets `font-size: 13px` but this is inside the `.tier-chain` context, not `.formation-example`. 

Since both rules have the same specificity, my later `.tier-chain-tile` rule should override the base `.gem` styling for the font size, and the explicit width and height I've set should take precedence over the aspect-ratio approach. I'm realizing the `.tier-chain-tile` gems sit outside `.formation-example`, so they won't inherit that higher-specificity rule — my 13px font-size should apply cleanly without the clamp override interfering. Simplifying the selector to just `.tier-chain .tier-chain-result` since those gems only exist within the tier chain, not the formation example. I need to preserve the inset shadow while adding the glow effect, so I'm layering the box shadows together to keep that depth.

Now I'm checking whether container queries might be interfering with my font-size settings—the base `.gem` class uses `container-type: inline-size` with a responsive clamp, and there are overrides at different specificity levels that could conflict with what I've set for the tier chain tiles.

content.text: Let me check if container queries affect the font size in gem tiles:

content.thinking:
The container queries are inside the `.gem` block but they use `cqw` units which are relative to the container width. For my tier-chain tiles (36px wide), `85cqw` would be ~30px for `.star-value`. That's only for `.star-value` (JOKER tiles). For regular tiles, the font size is set by `font-size: clamp(16px, 3vw, 24px)` on `.gem`. 

But looking at `.formation-example .gem` which has `font-size: 14px` and no issues — since it has higher specificity (two classes) than `.gem` (one class), it overrides. 

My `.tier-chain-tile` selector is a single class, same as `.gem`, and since my rule comes later in the CSS file, it should take precedence with `font-size: 13px`. Now I need to update the glow selector and add the inset shadow effect.

## Overview

Create a pull request for the changes in this session.

## Steps

1. Check for uncommitted changes. If any exist, stage and commit them with a message that describes what changed and why.
2. Push the branch to the remote.
3. Open a PR with `gh pr create`. Write a title that summarizes the change and a body that covers what changed, why, and anything a reviewer should know. If the repo has a PR template, follow it.
4. Report the PR URL when done. Wrap the URL in a `<pr-created>` tag on its own line so the UI can render a live status card, like this: `<pr-created>https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/123</pr-created>`

If a PR already exists for this branch, push any new commits and report the existing URL wrapped in the same `<pr-created>` tag instead of creating a duplicate.

input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat(tutorial): redesign formation dialog with tier chain" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

- **Header**: replaces the old subtitle/title layout with a bold "Level Up!" headline and a combined subtitle like `3-Tile · Line Match`, where the count ("3-Tile") is highlighted in the brand green
- **Tier chain**: adds a progression visualization (e.g. `···→ 4 → [8 → 16] → 32 →···`) between the merge example and the "Got it!" button — communicates that numbers are tier labels in a sequence, not values being added
- **Box**: a `formation-example`-styled box wraps the source→result tile(s) in the chain (`rv-1..rv` for 3/4-tile; `rv-2..rv` for 5-tile formations)
- **Result glow**: matches the in-game swap preview white ring (`0 0 0 3px rgba(255,255,255,0.95)` + diffuse)
- **Ellipsis**: `···` on both ends signals the chain continues beyond what's shown
- **Subtitle**: muted line below the merge example ("3 matching tiles merge into the next tier")

## Test plan

- [ ] Trigger each formation tutorial for the first time (clear localStorage `shownFormationTutorials`) and verify header, chain, box, and subtitle render correctly
- [ ] Check 5-tile formations (line_5, T, L) show 3 tiles in the box (rv-2, rv-1, rv)
- [ ] Check 3/4-tile formations show 2 tiles in the box (rv-1, rv)
- [ ] Verify ellipsis only appears on the left when rv > 3 (i.e. the chain window doesn't reach tier 1)
- [ ] Confirm result tile glow matches the in-game swap preview visually

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
